skill.name: expo-api-routes
skill.description: Guides developers through creating Expo Router API routes, handling requests, managing secrets, testing locally, and deploying to EAS Hosting.
skill.summary: Create and deploy Expo Router API routes with EAS Hosting patterns.
skill.author: expo

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many shell, network, filesystem, environment, and weak crypto patterns, but most are false positives from Markdown code fences, inline code, route examples, or legitimate server-side configuration guidance. Human review found no prompt injection or malicious behavior, but several copyable examples could lead to insecure implementations if used without additional hardening.

medium_findings.title: Overly Permissive CORS Example Allows Authorization Headers
medium_findings.description: The CORS example allows any origin while also allowing the Authorization header. If copied into a production API, this can expose authenticated endpoints to browser calls from untrusted origins unless additional authentication and origin checks are added.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code is a documentation example, not executable skill code, but the insecure CORS pattern is explicit and copyable. The risk depends on how users adapt the example in production.

medium_findings.title: External API Proxy Example Does Not Encode User Input
medium_findings.description: The weather proxy example interpolates a query parameter directly into an external API URL with an API key. A copied implementation could allow query manipulation and unreliable upstream requests unless the value is validated and encoded.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The host is hardcoded, so this is not clear SSRF, but direct interpolation of request-controlled input into a URL is visible. The issue is a realistic implementation risk for copied example code.

low_findings.title: Shell Command Examples Are Documentation, Not Hidden Execution
low_findings.description: The analyzer flagged many Markdown backticks and bash examples as command execution. These commands are visible installation, login, local testing, curl, and deployment examples rather than code that the skill runs automatically.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The relevant text is inside Markdown fences and no helper script or hidden executable file is present. The commands are ordinary Expo and HTTP testing workflows.

low_findings.title: Environment Variable Examples Are Legitimate Server-Side Guidance
low_findings.description: The skill demonstrates server-side use of environment variables for API keys and database credentials. This is appropriate for API routes, though the examples should remind users to avoid command history exposure and to use secret managers where available.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill explicitly says secrets belong server-side and warns never to commit local env files. No evidence shows exfiltration or collection of environment values by the skill itself.

low_findings.title: Secret Value Placeholder Appears In Command Example
low_findings.description: The EAS environment command uses a placeholder API key value in a shell command. The placeholder is not a real secret, but real values passed this way can appear in shell history or process listings.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The value shown is clearly a placeholder, which lowers severity. The command pattern can still teach a less safe secret entry method if copied with a real value.

low_findings.title: Relative Import Misclassified As Path Traversal
low_findings.description: The path traversal finding points to a TypeScript relative import from a route file to a utility module. It is not file access and does not read user-controlled paths.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged sequence appears only in an import path inside a code example. There is no filesystem API call or user input involved.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detection Is A False Positive
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography detections do not correspond to use of MD5, SHA-1, DES, or another weak primitive. The only crypto example uses SHA-256 through Web Crypto.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 258 through 262 shows SHA-256, which is not the weak algorithm category flagged by the analyzer. The other flagged lines are prose and status-code guidance, not cryptographic code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Copyable Production-Sensitive Examples Require Hardening
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill contains examples for CORS, environment variables, external API proxying, curl testing, and deployment commands. These are expected for the topic, but users should adapt them with validation, origin restrictions, and secret handling controls before production use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The patterns are visible in examples and align with common production risks. They are not evidence of malicious skill behavior.

content.user_title: Build Expo API Routes for EAS Hosting
content.value_statement: Expo apps often need server-side endpoints for secrets, validation, and external service calls. This skill provides practical Expo Router API route patterns for local testing and EAS Hosting deployment.
actual_capabilities: Explains when Expo Router API routes are appropriate for server-side work.
Shows file naming patterns for static and dynamic API routes.
Demonstrates GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, OPTIONS, headers, query parameters, and JSON bodies.
Covers server-side environment variables and deployment to EAS Hosting.
Lists EAS Hosting runtime limits and compatible Web API alternatives.
Provides examples for authentication middleware, client calls, and external API proxying.
limitations: Examples are guidance and are not a complete production security framework.
Authentication examples use placeholders and require real token verification.
Database examples are illustrative and do not include migrations or schema design.
Deployment steps assume an Expo project configured for EAS Hosting.

use_cases.title: Add Server-Side Endpoints to an Expo App
use_cases.description: Create route files that handle requests without exposing secrets in client code.

use_cases.title: Prepare API Routes for EAS Hosting
use_cases.description: Check runtime limits, environment variable setup, and deployment commands before shipping.

use_cases.title: Proxy Third-Party Services Securely
use_cases.description: Design endpoints that keep provider keys on the server and return controlled responses to clients.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Route
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a new endpoint.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to create a basic Expo Router API route for my app. Include the file path, HTTP method, and response shape.

prompt_templates.title: Handle Request Data
prompt_templates.scenario: Add query, header, or JSON body handling.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design an Expo API route that reads request data, validates required fields, and returns proper status codes.

prompt_templates.title: Add Server-Side Secrets
prompt_templates.scenario: Call an external service without exposing keys.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to build an Expo API route that calls a third-party API with a server-side environment variable. Include validation and safe error handling.

prompt_templates.title: Review an EAS Hosting API Design
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to review my Expo Router API route plan for EAS Hosting. Check runtime limits, authentication, CORS, secrets, and deployment concerns.

output_examples.input: I need an endpoint for submitting a contact form from an Expo app.
output_examples.output: A route plan with the correct app/api file path, POST handling, required field validation, success and error status codes, and notes for server-side secret storage.

output_examples.input: I want to deploy API routes to EAS Hosting.
output_examples.output: A deployment checklist covering EAS CLI setup, environment variables, runtime limitations, local testing, and production verification steps.

output_examples.input: I need to call a weather API without exposing my provider key.
output_examples.output: A proxy endpoint design that keeps the key server-side, validates the city parameter, encodes outbound query values, and returns a controlled response.

best_practices: Validate and sanitize every value from query strings, headers, and request bodies.
Keep secrets in server-side environment variables and avoid exposing them to client bundles.
Restrict CORS origins and authorization behavior for production APIs.
anti_patterns: Do not use API routes for public static data that can be fetched directly.
Do not copy placeholder authentication logic without real token verification.
Do not pass unencoded user input into external service URLs.

faq.question: What does this skill help build?
faq.answer: It helps build Expo Router API routes for request handling, secrets, external API calls, and EAS Hosting deployment.

faq.question: Does it generate a full backend application?
faq.answer: No. It provides route patterns and guidance, but you must add project-specific data models, authentication, and business logic.

faq.question: Can it help with database access?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains compatible serverless database options and shows a basic Turso-style client example.

faq.question: Does it cover local testing?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes Expo serving and curl-based testing patterns for local API route checks.

faq.question: Is it safe for secrets?
faq.answer: It recommends server-side environment variables, but production projects should use managed secrets and avoid unsafe shell history exposure.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: It is listed for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows that need Expo API route guidance.
